title: Mordheim: City of the Damned - Witch Hunters
anchor_summary: Add-on warband campaign for Mordheim: City of the Damned, preserving its core identity of turn-based tactical skirmishes, persistent squad progression, heavy unit customization, and punishing injury/permadeath management.

similar_games.title: Mordheim: City of the Damned
similar_games.why_similar: This DLC directly inherits the base game's turn-based warband combat, persistent roster growth, equipment management, and lethal campaign structure.

similar_games.title: Battle Brothers
similar_games.why_similar: Small-squad tactical battles, persistent mercenary management, injuries/death that matter, and long-term roster building make it a close gameplay neighbor.

similar_games.title: XCOM 2
similar_games.why_similar: Turn-based squad tactics with high-stakes missions, unit progression, equipment loadouts, and permanent soldier losses.

similar_games.title: Warhammer 40,000: Chaos Gate - Daemonhunters
similar_games.why_similar: Mission-based turn-based tactics centered on customized units, class abilities, and campaign persistence within a grim Warhammer setting.

similar_games.title: Necromunda: Underhive Wars
similar_games.why_similar: Another Games Workshop-style gang skirmish tactics game focused on warband composition, progression, and hazardous battlefield positioning.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: This title is clearly DLC, and its own description is additive rather than contradictory: it adds a new Witch Hunters warband, unit types, and a playable campaign. Per DLC guidance, the parent game's richer gameplay evidence should define core identity. The parent is explicitly described as a 'turn-based tactical game' where players lead warbands in lethal skirmishes, blending RPG elements, unit customization, gear, experience gain, missions, campaign play, and multiplayer battles. That strongly supports turn_based_tactics as primary, with tactical_rpg as secondary due to warband growth, customization, and persistent unit progression. The DLC text further reinforces party-based tactical combat through unit classes, weapons, skills, spellcasting, and campaign content, without indicating any shift to a different genre or mode.
